content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA with NNN integers. An array's score is defined as the bitwise AND of all its elements. You need to find the bitwise OR of the scores of all possible non-empty subarrays of AAA.

Furthermore, there are QQQ queries. Each query consists of two integers XXX and VVV. You need to change the value of the element at index XXX to VVV. After each query, you again need to find the bitwise OR of the scores of all possible non-empty subarrays.

See the example for more clarification.

###Input:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ - the size of the array and the number of queries, respectively.

The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,…,ANA_1,\ldots,A_NA1​,…,AN​.

Each of the next QQQ lines contains two space-separated integers XXX and VVV - the position and the new value of the query, respectively.

###Output:
For each test case print Q+1Q+1Q+1 lines. In the first line print the answer for the original array and in the next QQQ lines print the answer after every query.

###Constraints
1≤T≤1001 \le T \le 1001≤T≤100

1≤N,Q≤1051 \le N, Q \le 10^51≤N,Q≤105

0≤Ai≤231−10 \le A_i \le 2^{31}-10≤Ai​≤231−1

1≤X≤N1 \le X \le N1≤X≤N

0≤V≤231−10 \le V \le 2^{31}-10≤V≤231−1

The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10510^5105

The sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 10510^5105
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
The sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q.
- The second line contains N integers A1 … AN.
- Each of the next Q lines contains two integers X and V, meaning set A[X] = V (1‑based index).
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output Q+1 lines.
The first line is the answer for the original array, and each of the next Q lines is the answer after applying the corresponding query.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: For the original array, all possible subarrays and their scores are as follows.
AND(1)=1AND(1) = 1AND(1)=1, AND(2)=2AND(2) = 2AND(2)=2, AND(3)=3AND(3) = 3AND(3)=3, AND(1,2)=0AND(1,2) = 0AND(1,2)=0, AND(2,3)=2AND(2,3) = 2AND(2,3)=2, AND(1,2,3)=0AND(1,2,3) = 0AND(1,2,3)=0.
The bitwise OR of all possible subarray's score is OR(1,2,3,0,2,0)=3OR(1,2,3,0,2,0) = 3OR(1,2,3,0,2,0)=3.
After the first query new array will be [4,2,3][4,2,3][4,2,3] and the answer will be 777.
After the second query new array will be [4,2,0][4,2,0][4,2,0] and the answer will be 666.

solution.approach:
Observation: The AND of a subarray has a bit set only if every element of that subarray has the bit set. Therefore a bit appears in the OR of all subarray‑ANDs iff there exists at least one array element with that bit set. Consequently, the required value is simply the bitwise OR of all array elements.

We only need to maintain the global OR under point updates. Keep an array cnt[0..30] where cnt[b] is the number of elements whose b‑th bit is 1. When an element changes from old to new, for each bit b we decrement cnt[b] if old had the bit and increment if new has the bit. After the update the answer is recomputed as sum_{b where cnt[b]>0} (1<<b). Each update touches at most 31 bits, so the total complexity is O((N+Q)·31).
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) * 31) ≈ O(N + Q) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + 31) – the array itself plus the 31 counters
